topic.what: Meta-analysis of randomized controlled trials evaluating whether H. pylori eradication therapy reduces the incidence of gastric cancer in infected individuals.
topic.how: Pooled data from multiple RCTs comparing H. pylori eradication versus placebo or no treatment, reporting relative risk of gastric cancer development.
topic.why_matters: Establishes causal evidence that population-level screen-and-treat strategies for H. pylori can meaningfully reduce gastric cancer incidence, with a relative risk of approximately 0.54.

characteristics.what_it_measures: Relative risk of gastric cancer after H. pylori eradication compared to control
characteristics.vs_baseline: 46% relative reduction in gastric cancer incidence versus no eradication
characteristics.evidence: Pooled RCT data across multiple trials with consistent direction of effect
characteristics.context: Asymptomatic H. pylori-infected populations; effect may vary by baseline cancer risk and stage of gastric atrophy

good_for: Informing population-level screen-and-treat policies for H. pylori
Justifying eradication therapy in high-prevalence regions
Quantifying the number needed to treat (NNT) for gastric cancer prevention
not_for: Patients with already advanced gastric precancerous lesions where eradication benefit is uncertain
Regions with very low H. pylori prevalence where screening cost-effectiveness is poor
requires: Access to eradication therapy with acceptable resistance profiles

novelty: Provides the most comprehensive RCT-level evidence for the causal link between H. pylori eradication and gastric cancer risk reduction
Quantifies the effect size (RR ~0.54) with sufficient power to inform clinical guidelines
open_problems: Optimal timing of eradication relative to the precancerous cascade stage
Whether eradication benefit persists in patients who have already developed intestinal metaplasia or dysplasia
